items.q: What is a negative externality in environmental economics?
items.correct: A cost imposed on third parties not reflected in the market price, such as pollution.
items.misconception: A benefit the producer keeps for themselves.
items.random_wrong: A tax charged on all businesses.
items.why: Students confuse externalities with ordinary costs or taxes, missing that they fall on uninvolved parties.

items.q: Why can natural capital be 'used up' even though nature renews itself?
items.correct: Because harvesting beyond the natural income depletes the underlying stock faster than it regenerates.
items.misconception: Because natural capital is completely unlimited.
items.random_wrong: Because natural capital refers only to money in banks.
items.why: Learners assume nature is inexhaustible or misread 'capital' as purely financial.

items.q: Does GDP fully measure a country's environmental wellbeing?
items.correct: No; GDP ignores resource depletion and pollution, so it can rise while natural capital falls.
items.misconception: Yes; GDP captures all environmental costs.
items.why: Students assume economic growth figures automatically reflect environmental health.

items.q: The benefits people gain from nature, such as pollination and clean water, are called:
items.why: Correct answer: Ecosystem services. “Externalities” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Pollution from a factory that harms residents but is not reflected in its prices is an example of an:
items.why: Correct answer: Externality. “Ecosystem service” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The world's stock of natural resources viewed as economic assets is termed:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Natural capital. “Gross domestic product” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The 'polluter pays' principle states that the cost of pollution should fall on:
items.why: It's Those who cause it. “The general taxpayer” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
